topics.heading: CRUD Operations
points: **SELECT** — `SELECT col1, col2 FROM table WHERE condition ORDER BY col ASC/DESC LIMIT n OFFSET m`.
**INSERT** — `INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES ('John', 'john@mail.com')`. Bulk: `INSERT INTO ... SELECT ...`.
**UPDATE** — `UPDATE users SET name = 'Jane' WHERE id = 1`. Always use WHERE clause.
**DELETE** — `DELETE FROM users WHERE id = 1`. `TRUNCATE TABLE users` — faster, resets auto-increment.
**UPSERT** — MySQL: `INSERT ... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE`. PostgreSQL: `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE`.

topics.heading: Filtering & Sorting
points: **WHERE Clauses** — `=`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `BETWEEN`, `IN`, `LIKE`, `IS NULL`, `IS NOT NULL`.
**LIKE Pattern** — `%` any characters, `_` single character. `WHERE name LIKE 'J%'`.
**AND / OR / NOT** — Combine conditions. Use parentheses for precedence.
**ORDER BY** — `ORDER BY name ASC, created_at DESC`. `NULLS FIRST` / `NULLS LAST` (PostgreSQL).
**LIMIT & OFFSET** — Pagination: `LIMIT 20 OFFSET 40` (page 3, 20 per page).

topics.heading: Aggregations
points: **Aggregate Functions** — `COUNT()`, `SUM()`, `AVG()`, `MIN()`, `MAX()`. `COUNT(*)` counts all rows.
**GROUP BY** — `SELECT dept, COUNT(*) FROM emp GROUP BY dept`. Every non-aggregate column must be in GROUP BY.
**HAVING** — Filter groups. `SELECT dept, AVG(salary) FROM emp GROUP BY dept HAVING AVG(salary) > 50000`.
**DISTINCT** — `SELECT DISTINCT department FROM employees`. Removes duplicate rows.
**Window Functions** — `ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC)`. `RANK()`, `DENSE_RANK()`, `LAG()`, `LEAD()`.

topics.heading: JOIN Types
points: **INNER JOIN** — Returns matching rows from both tables. `SELECT * FROM orders o INNER JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id`.
**LEFT JOIN** — All rows from left table + matching from right. NULL if no match.
**RIGHT JOIN** — All rows from right table + matching from left. Less common.
**FULL OUTER JOIN** — All rows from both tables. NULL where no match. Not supported in MySQL.
**CROSS JOIN** — Cartesian product. Every row of A paired with every row of B. Rarely needed.

topics.heading: Self Join & Multi-Table
points: **Self Join** — Table joined with itself. `SELECT e.name, m.name AS manager FROM emp e JOIN emp m ON e.manager_id = m.id`.
**Multiple Joins** — Chain joins: `FROM orders o JOIN users u ON ... JOIN products p ON ...`.
**Join with Aggregation** — `SELECT u.name, COUNT(o.id) FROM users u LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id GROUP BY u.name`.
**Natural Join** — Joins on columns with same name. Implicit, fragile — avoid in production.

topics.heading: Subqueries
points: **Scalar Subquery** — Returns single value. `SELECT name, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE user_id = u.id) FROM users u`.
**IN Subquery** — `SELECT * FROM users WHERE id IN (SELECT user_id FROM orders WHERE total > 100)`.
**EXISTS** — `SELECT * FROM users u WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.user_id = u.id)`. Usually faster than IN.
**Correlated Subquery** — References outer query. Executes once per outer row. Consider JOIN instead.
**CTE (WITH clause)** — `WITH active_users AS (SELECT * FROM users WHERE active = true) SELECT * FROM active_users`.

topics.heading: Index Types
points: **B-Tree Index** — Default. Good for equality and range queries. `CREATE INDEX idx_name ON table(col)`.
**Composite Index** — Multi-column index. `CREATE INDEX idx ON table(col1, col2)`. Leftmost prefix rule.
**Unique Index** — Enforces uniqueness. Automatically created for PRIMARY KEY.
**Hash Index** — Only equality comparisons. Faster than B-Tree for `=`. Not for range queries.
**Full-Text Index** — Text search. `CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX idx ON articles(title, body)`.

topics.heading: Query Optimization
points: **EXPLAIN / EXPLAIN ANALYZE** — Show query execution plan. Look for: type (ALL = full scan), rows scanned, key used.
**Avoid SELECT *** — Fetch only needed columns. Reduces I/O, memory, and network transfer.
**Avoid Functions on Indexed Columns** — `WHERE YEAR(created_at) = 2024` won't use index. Use range instead.
**Use EXISTS over IN** — For large subqueries, EXISTS short-circuits.
**Batch Operations** — Batch inserts: `INSERT INTO table VALUES (...), (...), (...)`. Reduce round trips.

topics.heading: Connection Pooling
points: **HikariCP** — Default in Spring Boot. Fastest Java connection pool.
**Pool Sizing** — Formula: connections = (core_count * 2) + effective_spindle_count. Usually 10-20.
**Connection Leaks** — Enable leak detection: `spring.datasource.hikari.leak-detection-threshold=2000`.
**Timeouts** — `connection-timeout`, `idle-timeout`, `max-lifetime`. Configure appropriately.

topics.heading: Normal Forms
points: **1NF** — Atomic values, no repeating groups. Each cell contains a single value. Each row is unique.
**2NF** — 1NF + no partial dependencies. Every non-key column depends on the entire primary key.
**3NF** — 2NF + no transitive dependencies. Non-key columns depend only on the primary key.
**BCNF** — Stricter 3NF. Every determinant is a candidate key.
**When to Denormalize** — Read-heavy systems, reporting tables, caching layers. Trade storage for read speed.

topics.heading: Schema Design Best Practices
points: **Primary Keys** — Use surrogate keys (auto-increment ID or UUID). Avoid natural keys as PK.
**Foreign Keys** — Always define FK constraints. `ON DELETE CASCADE` or `ON DELETE SET NULL`.
**Naming Conventions** — Lowercase, snake_case. Plural table names. Singular column names.
**Soft Delete** — Add `deleted_at` timestamp instead of DELETE. `WHERE deleted_at IS NULL`.
**Timestamps** — Always include `created_at` and `updated_at`. Use database defaults.

topics.heading: ACID Properties
points: **Atomicity** — All or nothing. If any part fails, entire transaction rolls back.
**Consistency** — Database moves from one valid state to another. All constraints enforced.
**Isolation** — Concurrent transactions don't interfere. Each sees a consistent snapshot.
**Durability** — Once committed, data survives system crashes. Written to persistent storage.

topics.heading: Isolation Levels
points: **READ_UNCOMMITTED** — Dirty reads possible. Fastest but least safe.
**READ_COMMITTED** — No dirty reads. Only see committed data. Default in PostgreSQL.
**REPEATABLE_READ** — No dirty or non-repeatable reads. Default in MySQL/InnoDB.
**SERIALIZABLE** — Strictest. Full isolation. Slowest, most contention.
**Choosing Level** — Most apps: READ_COMMITTED. Financial: SERIALIZABLE.

topics.heading: Spring @Transactional
points: **Propagation** — REQUIRED (default), REQUIRES_NEW (always new), NESTED, SUPPORTS, NOT_SUPPORTED.
**Rollback Rules** — Default: rollback on `RuntimeException`. `@Transactional(rollbackFor = Exception.class)`.
**Read-Only** — `@Transactional(readOnly = true)`. Hint to JPA/DB. Optimizes flush mode.
**Timeout** — `@Transactional(timeout = 30)`. Transaction rolls back if exceeds timeout.
**Pitfall: Self-Invocation** — Calling `@Transactional` method from same class bypasses proxy.

topics.heading: Document Databases
points: **MongoDB** — JSON-like documents (BSON). Flexible schema. Collections instead of tables.
**Use Cases** — Content management, product catalogs, user profiles, real-time analytics.
**Spring Data MongoDB** — `@Document`, `MongoRepository`. `MongoTemplate` for complex queries.
**Indexing** — `@Indexed`, compound indexes, text indexes, TTL indexes for auto-expiry.

topics.heading: Key-Value & Cache
points: **Redis** — In-memory data structure store. Sub-millisecond latency. Strings, lists, sets, hashes, sorted sets.
**Use Cases** — Caching, session storage, rate limiting, leaderboards, pub/sub, distributed locks.
**Spring Data Redis** — `RedisTemplate`, `@Cacheable`, `@CacheEvict`.
**TTL & Eviction** — Set expiry: `EXPIRE key seconds`. Policies: `allkeys-lru`, `volatile-lru`, `noeviction`.

topics.heading: When SQL vs NoSQL
points: **Choose SQL** — Complex queries/joins, strong consistency (ACID), well-defined schema, transactional systems.
**Choose NoSQL** — Flexible schema, horizontal scaling, specific access patterns, high write throughput.
**Polyglot Persistence** — Use different databases for different needs. SQL for orders, Redis for cache, MongoDB for logs.
**CAP Theorem** — Choose 2 of 3: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance.
